questions.question: A soccer ball has a mass of $400\text{ g}$ and is moving at $25\text{ m/s}$. Calculate its momentum in SI units.
questions.explanation:
First, convert mass to kg: $400\text{ g} = 0.4\text{ kg}$. Then $p = 0.4 \times 25 = 10\text{ kg m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the mass in grams directly to get^10000.

questions.question: A bus has a mass of $8000\text{ kg}$ and a momentum of $88000\text{ kg m/s}$. What is the speed of the bus?
questions.explanation:
Rearranging $p = mv$ gives $v = p/m^2 88000 / 8000 = 11\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the numbers ($704$ million) instead of dividing.

questions.question: A car travelling at $12\text{ m/s}$ has a momentum of $14400\text{ kg m/s}$. Calculate the mass of the car.
questions.explanation:
Rearranging $p = mv$ gives $m = p/v$. $14400 / 12 = 1200\text{ kg}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying velocity and momentum ($172800$).

questions.question: Two objects, A and B, are moving at the same velocity. Object A is more massive than object B. Which statement is correct?
options: Object A has more momentum than object B
Object B has more momentum than object A
They have the same momentum because their velocities are equal
Neither object has momentum because the force is not stated
questions.explanation:
Since momentum is the product of mass and velocity, if velocity is constant, the object with the greater mass will have the greater momentum. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking that same speed means same momentum regardless of mass.

questions.question: A car of mass $1000\text{ kg}$ is travelling at $18\text{ km/h}$. Calculate its momentum in $\text{kg m/s}$.
questions.explanation:
First convert $18\text{ km/h}$ to $\text{m/s}$: $18 \times (1000/3600) = 5\text{ m/s}$. Then $p = 1000 \times 5 = 5000\text{ kg m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the speed in $\text{km/h}$ directly ($18000$).

questions.question: A $0.4\text{ kg}$ ball is kicked from rest and reaches a speed of $5\text{ m/s}$. Calculate the impulse given to the ball.
questions.explanation:
Impulse is the change in momentum: $Delta(mv) = 0.4\text{ kg} \times (5 - 0)\text{ m/s} = 2.0\text{ kg m/s}$ (or $N s ).$

**Common mistake:** Using only the speed (5.0$) or mass ($0.4$) as the impulse.$

questions.question: A ball receives an impulse of $2.0\text{ N s}$. If the average force applied was $100\text{ N}$, how long was the contact time?
questions.explanation:
Using $FDelta t = Impulse , so Delta t = Impulse / F = 2.0 / 100 = 0.02\text{ s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the numbers ($200$) instead of dividing.

questions.question: A car of mass $1000\text{ kg}$ is travelling at $5\text{ m/s}$ and comes to a complete stop. What is the change in its momentum?
questions.explanation:
Change in momentum $= mv - mu = 1000 \times (0 - 5) = -5000\text{ kg m/s}$. Magnitude is $5000\text{ kg m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing mass by velocity ($200$).

questions.question: Object X and Object Y undergo the same change in momentum. Object X stops in $0.1\text{ s}$, while Object Y stops in $0.5\text{ s}$. How do the forces acting on them compare?
options: $The force on X is 5 \times greater than on Y$
$The force on Y is 5 \times greater than on X$
The forces are equal because the impulse is the same
The force on X is half that of Y
questions.explanation:
Since $F = Delta p / t$, if time is $1$ \times smaller ($0.1$ vs $0.5$), the force must be^5 \times larger. 

**Common mistake:** Assuming same impulse means same force regardless of time.

questions.question: Which equation correctly represents the relationship between Force ($F$), mass ($m ), change in velocity (Delta v$), and time ($t$)?
questions.explanation:
This is derived from $F = Delta p / t where Delta p = m Delta v$. So F = m Delta v / t2

**Common mistake:** Placing time in the numerator or ignoring the division entirely.

questions.question: Two ice skaters, A ($80\text{ kg}$) and B ($50\text{ kg}$), are stationary. They push each other off. Skater A moves at $0.5\text{ m/s}$. What is the magnitude of Skater B's velocity?
questions.explanation:
Initial momentum is zero. After pushing off: $m_A v_A + m_B v_B = 0 implies (80 \times 0.5) + (50 \times v_B) = 0 implies 40 = -50 v_B$, so $|v_B| = 0.8\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking they move at the same speed regardless of mass.

questions.question: A ball A ($0.12\text{ kg}$) moves at $0.40\text{ m/s}$ and hits stationary ball B ($0.09\text{ kg}$). After collision, ball B moves forward at $0.40\text{ m/s}$. Calculate the velocity of ball A.
questions.explanation:
Total before $= 0.12 \times 0.4 = 0.048$. Total after $= (0.12 \times v) + (0.09 \times 0.4) = 0.12v + 0.036$. $0.048 = 0.12v + 0.036 implies 0.012 = 0.12v implies v = 0.10\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Assuming Ball A stops completely.

questions.question: A car of mass $1200\text{ kg}$ travelling at $8.0\text{ m/s}$ hits a stationary lorry of mass $2800\text{ kg}$. If they stick together, what is their speed immediately after collision?
questions.explanation:
Total before $= 1200 \times 8 = 9600\text{ kg m/s}$. Combined mass $= 1200 + 2800 = 4000\text{ kg}$. Speed $= 9600 / 4000 = 2.4\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using only the lorry's mass to calculate the final speed.

questions.question: Two identical balls collide. Ball X is moving at $2\text{ m/s}$ and Ball Y is at rest. If the collision is elastic and Ball X comes to a stop, what is the velocity of Ball Y?
questions.explanation:
Since the masses are identical, for momentum to be conserved ($m \times 2 = m \times v$), the second ball must take on the entire initial velocity of the first. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the velocity is shared ($1\text{ m/s}$ each).

questions.question: Object P moves East with $5\text{ kg m/s}$ momentum. Object Q moves West with $5\text{ kg m/s}$ momentum. What is the total momentum of the system?
questions.explanation:
Momentum is a vector. Eastern direction is positive and Western is negative: $+5 + (-5) = 0$. 

**Common mistake:** Adding them as scalars to get^10.
